Let's discover Docusaurus in less than 5 minutes.
An online mathematical encyclopedia devoted to Deriving and exploring mathematics beyond the classroom for high school students. All Things Are Derivable

Our mission is to make mathematical reasoning transparent, tangible, and telling through Derivation while offering additional pathways for inquisitive and motivated students to explore mathematics with greter rigor and depth.
This project aims to demystify mathematical Deriving and to foster deeper curiosity through clear and careful reasoning.

Throughout this project, Derivation is treated as a proper noun, referring to the process of obtain a result through meticulous, step-by-step calculation. As the main theme of Art of Derivation, all words related to Derivation (such as Derive, Deriving) are highlighted in the main theme color. Some closely related terms, though not strictly equivalent, including Proof, Justification, and Logical Argument, all of which are integral to the idea of Derivation presented in this project. My understanding of Derivation was mainly developed from my AP Physics C: Mechanics course, where the Free Response Questions frequently require student to Derive expressions from fundamental definitions or equations in the provided equations sheet.
This project is built on Docusaurus and deployed on GitHub Pages. Below are detailed format information.

All documents are stored as Markdown files and use either the .md or .mdx extension. All document file names are capitalized and connected using hyphens (-). All documents are stored under the docs directory, organized by sidebars.js. All referenced images (such as .jpg, .png, .svg) are stored in the static/img directory.
All documents start with the front matter to ensure the consistent format and navigation. More additional details, refer to Markdown Features | Docusaurus.
All mathematical expressions are rendered using KaTeX with remark-math and follow standard LaTeX\LaTeXLATE‚ÄãX syntax.

All Derivation processes are placed in collapsible blocks, implemented using <details>+<summary>. All Derivation processes are organized using the align* enviornment. All Derivation processes are completed with \blacksquare (‚ñÝ\blacksquare‚ñÝ), indicating Q.E.D. (quod erat demonstrandum), meaning "that which was to be demonstrated".

Note The Gray Admonition provides extra and unnecessary notes and information. Definition The Green Admonition provides definition about the given topic. Property, Identity, Solution... The Blue Admonition provides Properties, Identities, Solutions, and so on that is NOT designed to show the collapsable Derivation process. Derivation The collapsable Admonition provides detailed process for Derivation Question The Orange Admonition highlights the question for examples. Warning The Red Admonition provides information that is important to know.

The theme color of this project is Indigo (#4b0082) in light mode and Magenta (#ff61ff) in dark mode. The theme color is exclusively reserved for Derivation and its related concepts, rather than decorative styling.
The idea of the favicon came from ‚àµ\because‚àµ ($\because$) and ‚à¥\therefore‚à¥ ($\therefore$).

All documents are organized in a logical sequence; following the given order is strongly recommended. Before viewing the full derivation through the collapsable bar, attempt to work through each step independently using pen and paper. Detailed solutions are provided in the collapsible sections for verification and deeper understanding. Maintaining a dedicated notebook for your own derivations is highly encouraged.

My teacher back then found a really sophisticated way through hyperbolic trigonometry substitution, and everyone was stunned by the complexity, but I came up with a simple algebraic transformation. Substitution is the most common integral technique in elementary calculus. Recall what we mentioned in common integrals that we use uuu and du\mathrm{d}udu instead of xxx and dx\mathrm{d}xdx, and this is because uuu may represent an entire function, not just a single variable. We will tackle with this more specifically in this section.

We will start with this integral question: Question ‚à´3x2sin‚Å°x3 dx= ?\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\ ?‚à´3x2sinx3 dx= ? Note that there is an product, which is beyond all common integrals, so we need to simplify to an easier form. An example is below: Solution Let: u=x3u=x^3u=x3 dudx=3x2du=3x2 dx\begin{align*} \frac{\mathrm{d}u}{\mathrm{d}x}&=3x^2\\ \color{red}{\mathrm{d}u}&=\color{red}{3x^2\ \mathrm{d}x}\\ \end{align*}dxdu‚Äãdu‚Äã=3x2=3x2 dx‚Äã Plug in back to the question: ‚à´3x2sin‚Å°x3 dx=‚à´sin‚Å°u du=‚à´sin‚Å°u du=‚àícos‚Å°u+C=‚àícos‚Å°x3+C\begin{align*} \int{\color{red}{3x^2}}\sin{x^3}\ \color{red}{\mathrm{d}x}&=\int{\sin{u}}\ \color{red}{\mathrm{d}u}\\ &=\int{\sin{u}}\ \mathrm{d}u\\ &=-\cos{u}+C\\ &=-\cos{x^3}+C \end{align*}‚à´3x2sinx3 dx‚Äã=‚à´sinu du=‚à´sinu du=‚àícosu+C=‚àícosx3+C‚Äã Hence: ‚à´3x2sin‚Å°x3 dx=‚àícos‚Å°x3+C‚ñÝ\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=-\cos{x^3}+C \tag*{$\blacksquare$}‚à´3x2sinx3 dx=‚àícosx3+C‚ñÝ By replacing the variable xxx into a function of uuu, we can simplify the overall question to a common integral. As you can see, this is a method we inversely use the Chain Rule, and generally, it is called: The substitution rule Given a differenciable function u=g(x)u=g(x)u=g(x), and fff is continuous on its interval, then: ‚à´f(g(x))g‚Ä≤(x) dx=‚à´f(u) du\int{f\left(g\left(x\right)\right)g'(x)}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int{f\left(u\right)}\ \mathrm{d}u‚à´f(g(x))g‚Ä≤(x) dx=‚à´f(u) du Definite Integral In the case of Definite Integral, the bound should also changes, as: ‚à´abf(g(x))g‚Ä≤(x) dx=‚à´g(a)g(b)f(u) du\int_{a}^{b}{f\left(g\left(x\right)\right)g'(x)}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int_{g(a)}^{g(b)}{f\left(u\right)}\ \mathrm{d}u‚à´ab‚Äãf(g(x))g‚Ä≤(x) dx=‚à´g(a)g(b)‚Äãf(u) du

Direct Substitution is a method created by the author himself. It simplify the process of substitution without write out uuu and du\mathrm{d}udu. I will show this with an example: ‚à´3x2sin‚Å°x3 dx= ?\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\ ?‚à´3x2sinx3 dx= ? This time, let's try what I called Direct Substituton: Direct Substitution Approach After analyze, we want to substitute x3x^3x3. All we need to do is substitude dx\mathrm{d}xdx to a fraction where the top is dx3\mathrm{d}x^3dx3 and the bottom is the derivative of x3x^3x3 based on xxx, as: ‚à´3x2sin‚Å°x3 dx=‚à´3x2sin‚Å°x3 d(x3)3x2=‚à´sin‚Å°x3 d(x3)\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int{\cancel{3x^2}\sin{x^3}}\ \frac{\mathrm{d}(x^3)}{\cancel{3x^2}}=\int{\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}(x^3)‚à´3x2sinx3 dx=‚à´3x2sinx3 3x2d(x3)‚Äã=‚à´sinx3 d(x3) And all we need is to consider x3x^3x3 as uuu and integrate normally: ‚üπ ‚à´sin‚Å°x3 d(x3)=‚àícos‚Å°x3+C\implies \int{\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}(x^3)=-\cos{x^3}+C‚üπ‚à´sinx3 d(x3)=‚àícosx3+C More importantly, for definite integral, it doesn't need to change bounds, as: ‚à´ab3x2sin‚Å°x3 dx=‚à´absin‚Å°x3 d(x3)=‚àícos‚Å°x3‚à£ab=‚àícos‚Å°b3+cos‚Å°a3\int_{a}^{b}3x^2\sin{x^3}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int_{a}^{b}{\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}(x^3)=-\cos{x^3}\big|_{a}^{b}=-\cos{b^3}+\cos{a^3}‚à´ab‚Äã3x2sinx3 dx=‚à´ab‚Äãsinx3 d(x3)=‚àícosx3‚Äãab‚Äã=‚àícosb3+cosa3 Direct Substitution In general, if we want to substitude ax+bax+bax+b or term that can cancel the other one, we may use direct substitution ‚à´f(x)g(x)‚èüSubstitute dx=‚à´f(x)g(x) d(g(x))g‚Ä≤(x)\int{{f(x)}\underbrace{g(x)}_{\text{Substitute}}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int{f(x)g(x)}\ \frac{\mathrm{d}(g(x))}{g'(x)} ‚à´f(x)Substituteg(x)‚Äã‚Äã dx=‚à´f(x)g(x) g‚Ä≤(x)d(g(x))‚Äã

If Cevians ADADAD, BEBEBE, CFCFCF all pass through pint OOO, then according to Italian mathematician Giovanni Ceva, we have Ceva's Theorem says: AD‚ÄæDB‚Äæ‚ãÖBE‚ÄæEC‚Äæ‚ãÖCF‚ÄæFA‚Äæ=1 \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BE}}{\overline{EC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CF}}{\overline{FA}}=1DBAD‚Äã‚ãÖECBE‚Äã‚ãÖFACF‚Äã=1 Where AD‚Äæ\overline{AD}AD represents the length of segment ADADAD Derivation Notice that the segment AD{AD}AD and segment DB{DB}DB share a same base ABABAB. ‚üπ AD‚ÄæDB‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥AOD][‚ñ≥BOD]=[‚ñ≥ACD][‚ñ≥BCD]‚à¥AD‚ÄæDB‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥ACD]‚àí[‚ñ≥AOD][‚ñ≥BCD]‚àí[‚ñ≥BOD]=[‚ñ≥ACO][‚ñ≥BCO] \begin{align*} \implies \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}&=\frac{[\triangle AOD]}{[\triangle BOD]}=\frac{[\triangle ACD]}{[\triangle BCD]}\\ \therefore \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}&=\frac{[\triangle ACD]-[\triangle AOD]}{[\triangle BCD]-[\triangle BOD]}= \frac{[\triangle ACO]}{[\triangle BCO]} \end{align*}‚üπDBAD‚Äã‚à¥DBAD‚Äã‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BOD][‚ñ≥AOD]‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BCD][‚ñ≥ACD]‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BCD]‚àí[‚ñ≥BOD][‚ñ≥ACD]‚àí[‚ñ≥AOD]‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BCO][‚ñ≥ACO]‚Äã‚Äã Similarly, we may also et: BE‚ÄæEC‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥BAO][‚ñ≥CAO], CF‚ÄæFA‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥CBO][‚ñ≥ABO] \frac{\overline{BE}}{\overline{EC}}=\frac{[\triangle BAO]}{[\triangle CAO]}, \ \frac{\overline{CF}}{\overline{FA}}=\frac{[\triangle CBO]}{[\triangle ABO]}ECBE‚Äã=[‚ñ≥CAO][‚ñ≥BAO]‚Äã, FACF‚Äã=[‚ñ≥ABO][‚ñ≥CBO]‚Äã Hence: AD‚ÄæDB‚Äæ‚ãÖBE‚ÄæEC‚Äæ‚ãÖCF‚ÄæFA‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥ACO][‚ñ≥BCO]‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥ABO][‚ñ≥ACO]‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥BCO][‚ñ≥ABO]=1‚ñÝ \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BE}}{\overline{EC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CF}}{\overline{FA}}= \frac{[\triangle ACO]}{[\triangle BCO]}\cdot \frac{[\triangle ABO]}{[\triangle ACO]}\cdot \frac{[\triangle BCO]}{[\triangle ABO]}=1 \tag*{$\blacksquare$}DBAD‚Äã‚ãÖECBE‚Äã‚ãÖFACF‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BCO][‚ñ≥ACO]‚Äã‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥ACO][‚ñ≥ABO]‚Äã‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥ABO][‚ñ≥BCO]‚Äã=1‚ñÝ Note: [‚ñ≥AOD][\triangle AOD][‚ñ≥AOD] represents the area of ‚ñ≥AOD\triangle AOD‚ñ≥AOD

Heron's Theorem Given a ‚ñ≥ABC\triangle ABC‚ñ≥ABC with the side length aaa, bbb, and ccc, then, the area is: S‚ñ≥ABC=p(p‚àía)(p‚àíb)(p‚àíc) S_{\triangle{ABC}}=\sqrt{p(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)}S‚ñ≥ABC‚Äã=p(p‚àía)(p‚àíb)(p‚àíc)‚Äã where p=a+b+c2p=\frac{a+b+c}{2}p=2a+b+c‚Äã, as half of the semiperimeter of ‚ñ≥ABC\triangle ABC‚ñ≥ABC Qin Jiushao's formula Alternatively, here is an equivalent form of Heron's formula S‚ñ≥ABC=14[a2c2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c22)2] S_{\triangle{ABC}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\left[a^2c^2-\left(\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2}\right)^2 \right]}S‚ñ≥ABC‚Äã=41‚Äã[a2c2‚àí(2a2‚àíb2+c2‚Äã)2]‚Äã Derivation Let BH‚ä•ACBH \perp ACBH‚ä•AC; HHH is a point on ACACAC. From the Pythagorean Theorem, we get: a2‚àíx2=b2‚àí(c‚àíx)2a2‚àíx2=b2‚àíc2+2cx‚àíx22cx=a2‚àíb2+c2x=a2‚àíb2+c22c\begin{align*} a^2-x^2&=b^2-(c-x)^2\\ a^2-x^2&=b^2-c^2+2cx-x^2\\ 2cx&=a^2-b^2+c^2\\ x&=\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2c} \end{align*}a2‚àíx2a2‚àíx22cxx‚Äã=b2‚àí(c‚àíx)2=b2‚àíc2+2cx‚àíx2=a2‚àíb2+c2=2ca2‚àíb2+c2‚Äã‚Äã S‚ñ≥ABC=12AC‚ãÖBH=12a‚ãÖc2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c22c)2=14[a2c2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c22)2](Qin Jiushao‚Äôs formula)=116[4a2c2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c2)2]=(2ac)2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c2)216=(2ac+a2‚àíb2+c2)(2ac‚àía2+b2‚àíc2)16=[(a+c)2‚àíb2][b2‚àí(a‚àíc)2]16=(a+c+b)(a+c‚àíb)(b+a‚àíc)(b‚àía+c)16=a+b+c2‚àía+b+c2a‚àíb+c2a+b‚àíc2=a+b+c2(a+b+c2‚àía)(a+b+c2‚àíb)(a+b+c2‚àíc) \begin{align*} S_{\triangle{ABC}}&=\frac{1}{2}AC\cdot BH \\ &=\frac{1}{2}a\cdot \sqrt{c^2-\left(\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2c}\right)^2}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\left[a^2c^2-\left(\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2}\right)^2 \right]} &&\text{(Qin Jiushao's formula)}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{1}{16}[4a^2c^2-(a^2-b^2+c^2)^2]}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{(2ac)^2-(a^2-b^2+c^2)^2}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{(2ac+a^2-b^2+c^2)(2ac-a^2+b^2-c^2)}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{[(a+c)^2-b^2][b^2-(a-c)^2]}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{(a+c+b)(a+c-b)(b+a-c)(b-a+c)}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{a+b+c}{2}\frac{-a+b+c}{2}\frac{a-b+c}{2}\frac{a+b-c}{2}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{a+b+c}{2}\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-a\right)\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-b\right)\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-c\right)}\\ \end{align*}S‚ñ≥ABC‚Äã‚Äã=21‚ÄãAC‚ãÖBH=21‚Äãa‚ãÖc2‚àí(2ca2‚àíb2+c2‚Äã)2‚Äã=41‚Äã[a2c2‚àí(2a2‚àíb2+c2‚Äã)2]‚Äã=161‚Äã[4a2c2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c2)2]‚Äã=16(2ac)2‚àí(a2‚àíb2+c2)2‚Äã‚Äã=16(2ac+a2‚àíb2+c2)(2ac‚àía2+b2‚àíc2)‚Äã‚Äã=16[(a+c)2‚àíb2][b2‚àí(a‚àíc)2]‚Äã‚Äã=16(a+c+b)(a+c‚àíb)(b+a‚àíc)(b‚àía+c)‚Äã‚Äã=2a+b+c‚Äã2‚àía+b+c‚Äã2a‚àíb+c‚Äã2a+b‚àíc‚Äã‚Äã=2a+b+c‚Äã(2a+b+c‚Äã‚àía)(2a+b+c‚Äã‚àíb)(2a+b+c‚Äã‚àíc)‚Äã‚Äã(Qin Jiushao‚Äôs formula) Now, let p=a+b+c2p=\frac{a+b+c}{2}p=2a+b+c‚Äã, then: S‚ñ≥ABC=p(p‚àía)(p‚àíb)(p‚àíc)‚ñÝ S_{\triangle{ABC}}=\sqrt{p(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}S‚ñ≥ABC‚Äã=p(p‚àía)(p‚àíb)(p‚àíc)‚Äã‚ñÝ

Since there are some triangles share the same base and altitude: AM‚ÄæMB‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥ALM][‚ñ≥BLM],BL‚ÄæLC‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥BLM][‚ñ≥CLM],CN‚ÄæNA‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥CLM][‚ñ≥ALM] \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}=\frac{[\triangle ALM]}{[\triangle BLM]}, \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}}=\frac{[\triangle BLM]}{[\triangle CLM]}, \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}=\frac{[\triangle CLM]}{[\triangle ALM]}MBAM‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BLM][‚ñ≥ALM]‚Äã,LCBL‚Äã=[‚ñ≥CLM][‚ñ≥BLM]‚Äã,NACN‚Äã=[‚ñ≥ALM][‚ñ≥CLM]‚Äã Hence: AM‚ÄæMB‚Äæ‚ãÖBL‚ÄæLC‚Äæ‚ãÖCN‚ÄæNA‚Äæ=[‚ñ≥ALM][‚ñ≥BLM]‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥BLM][‚ñ≥CLM]‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥CLM][‚ñ≥ALM]=1‚ñÝ \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}=\frac{[\triangle ALM]}{[\triangle BLM]}\cdot \frac{[\triangle BLM]}{[\triangle CLM]} \cdot \frac{[\triangle CLM]}{[\triangle ALM]} =1 \tag*{$\blacksquare$}MBAM‚Äã‚ãÖLCBL‚Äã‚ãÖNACN‚Äã=[‚ñ≥BLM][‚ñ≥ALM]‚Äã‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥CLM][‚ñ≥BLM]‚Äã‚ãÖ[‚ñ≥ALM][‚ñ≥CLM]‚Äã=1‚ñÝ Note: [‚ñ≥AOD][\triangle AOD][‚ñ≥AOD] represents the area of ‚ñ≥AOD\triangle AOD‚ñ≥AOD Derivation 2: Trigonometry Let ‚àÝAMN=‚àÝBML=Œ±\angle AMN=\angle BML=\alpha‚àÝAMN=‚àÝBML=Œ±, ‚àÝANM=Œ≤\angle ANM=\beta‚àÝANM=Œ≤, ‚àÝBLM=Œ≥\angle BLM=\gamma‚àÝBLM=Œ≥. According to the Law of Sines, in ‚ñ≥AMN\triangle AMN‚ñ≥AMN: NA‚Äæsin‚Å°Œ±=AM‚Äæsin‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ AM‚ÄæNA‚Äæ=sin‚Å°Œ≤sin‚Å°Œ±\frac{\overline{NA}}{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{{\overline{AM}}}{\sin{\beta}} \implies \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{NA}}= \frac{\sin{\beta}}{\sin{\alpha}}sinŒ±NA‚Äã=sinŒ≤AM‚Äã‚üπNAAM‚Äã=sinŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äã Similarly, in ‚ñ≥BLM\triangle{BLM}‚ñ≥BLM and ‚ñ≥CLM\triangle{CLM}‚ñ≥CLM, we get: BL‚ÄæMB‚Äæ=sin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≥, CN‚ÄæLC‚Äæ=sin‚Å°Œ≥sin‚Å°(180¬∞‚àíŒ≤)=sin‚Å°Œ≥sin‚Å°Œ≤\frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{MB}}= \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{\sin{\gamma}},\ \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{LC}}= \frac{\sin{\gamma}}{\sin{(180\degree-\beta)}}= \frac{\sin{\gamma}}{\sin{\beta}}MBBL‚Äã=sinŒ≥sinŒ±‚Äã, LCCN‚Äã=sin(180¬∞‚àíŒ≤)sinŒ≥‚Äã=sinŒ≤sinŒ≥‚Äã Hence: AM‚ÄæMB‚Äæ‚ãÖBL‚ÄæLC‚Äæ‚ãÖCN‚ÄæNA‚Äæ=AM‚ÄæNA‚Äæ‚ãÖBL‚ÄæMB‚Äæ‚ãÖCN‚ÄæLC‚Äæ=sin‚Å°Œ≤sin‚Å°Œ±‚ãÖsin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≥‚ãÖsin‚Å°Œ≥sin‚Å°Œ≤=1‚ñÝ\frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}= \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{NA}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{LC}}= \frac{\sin{\beta}}{\sin{\alpha}}\cdot \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{\sin{\gamma}} \cdot \frac{\sin{\gamma}}{\sin{\beta}}=1 \tag*{$\blacksquare$}MBAM‚Äã‚ãÖLCBL‚Äã‚ãÖNACN‚Äã=NAAM‚Äã‚ãÖMBBL‚Äã‚ãÖLCCN‚Äã=sinŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äã‚ãÖsinŒ≥sinŒ±‚Äã‚ãÖsinŒ≤sinŒ≥‚Äã=1‚ñÝ

Law of Cosines For a random ‚ñ≥ABC\triangle ABC‚ñ≥ABC, aaa, bbb, and ccc are the length of the sides of the triangle, and respectively, ‚àÝA\angle A‚àÝA, ‚àÝB\angle B‚àÝB, and ‚àÝC\angle C‚àÝC are the opposite angles, then the Law of Cosines points out: c2=a2+b2‚àí2abcos‚Å°‚àÝC c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos{\angle C} c2=a2+b2‚àí2abcos‚àÝC And similarly: a2=b2+c2‚àí2bccos‚Å°‚àÝA a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos{\angle A}a2=b2+c2‚àí2bccos‚àÝAb2=a2+c2‚àí2accos‚Å°‚àÝB b^2=a^2+c^2-2ac\cos{\angle B}b2=a2+c2‚àí2accos‚àÝB Derivation Draw a prependicular line from point C to side ABABAB and intersect with it at point D, and we have: c=BD‚Äæ+AD‚Äæ=acos‚Å°‚àÝB+bcos‚Å°‚àÝA c=\overline{BD}+\overline{AD}=a\cos{\angle B}+b\cos{\angle A}c=BD+AD=acos‚àÝB+bcos‚àÝA Times ccc on both side: c2=accos‚Å°‚àÝB+bccos‚Å°‚àÝA c^2=ac\cos{\angle B}+bc\cos{\angle A}c2=accos‚àÝB+bccos‚àÝA By the same reasoning: a2=abcos‚Å°‚àÝC+accos‚Å°‚àÝB a^2=ab\cos{\angle C}+ac\cos{\angle B}a2=abcos‚àÝC+accos‚àÝBb2=abcos‚Å°‚àÝC+bccos‚Å°‚àÝA b^2=ab\cos{\angle C}+bc\cos{\angle A}b2=abcos‚àÝC+bccos‚àÝA ‚à¥accos‚Å°‚àÝB=a2‚àíabcos‚Å°‚àÝC, bccos‚Å°‚àÝA=b2‚àíabcos‚Å°‚àÝC\therefore ac\cos{\angle B}= a^2-ab\cos{\angle C}, \ bc\cos{\angle A}= b^2-ab\cos{\angle C}‚à¥accos‚àÝB=a2‚àíabcos‚àÝC, bccos‚àÝA=b2‚àíabcos‚àÝC‚à¥c2=accos‚Å°‚àÝB+bccos‚Å°‚àÝA=(a2‚àíabcos‚Å°‚àÝC)+(b2‚àíabcos‚Å°‚àÝC)=a2+b2‚àí2abcos‚Å°‚àÝC\therefore c^2=ac\cos{\angle B}+bc\cos{\angle A}= (a^2-ab\cos{\angle C})+(b^2-ab\cos{\angle C})=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos{\angle C} ‚à¥c2=accos‚àÝB+bccos‚àÝA=(a2‚àíabcos‚àÝC)+(b2‚àíabcos‚àÝC)=a2+b2‚àí2abcos‚àÝC By draw the perpendicular line from different direction, similarly: a2=b2+c2‚àí2bccos‚Å°‚àÝA a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos{\angle A}a2=b2+c2‚àí2bccos‚àÝAb2=a2+c2‚àí2accos‚Å°‚àÝB‚ñÝ b^2=a^2+c^2-2ac\cos{\angle B} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}b2=a2+c2‚àí2accos‚àÝB‚ñÝ

Note that ‚àÝA\angle A‚àÝA is an inscribed angle, while ‚àÝBOC\angle BOC‚àÝBOC is the central angle subtending BC‚å¢\overset{\frown}{BC}BC‚å¢, which means: ‚àÝA=12‚àÝBOC\angle A=\frac{1}{2}\angle BOC‚àÝA=21‚Äã‚àÝBOC‚àµOB=OC=R, OD‚ä•BC‚à¥‚àÝBOD=‚àÝCOD=12‚àÝBOC=‚àÝA‚à¥sin‚Å°‚àÝA=sin‚Å°‚àÝCOD=CDOC=12aR ‚üπ asin‚Å°‚àÝA=2R \begin{align*} &\because OB=OC=R, \ OD\perp BC \\ &\therefore \angle BOD=\angle COD= \frac{1}{2}\angle BOC=\angle A\\ &\therefore \sin{\angle A}=\sin{\angle COD}=\frac{CD}{OC}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}a}{R}\\ &\implies \frac{a}{\sin {\angle A}}= 2R \end{align*}‚Äã‚àµOB=OC=R, OD‚ä•BC‚à¥‚àÝBOD=‚àÝCOD=21‚Äã‚àÝBOC=‚àÝA‚à¥sin‚àÝA=sin‚àÝCOD=OCCD‚Äã=R21‚Äãa‚Äã‚üπsin‚àÝAa‚Äã=2R‚Äã Similarly, from other directions, we may also get: bsin‚Å°‚àÝB=2R, csin‚Å°‚àÝC=2R\frac{b}{\sin {\angle B}}= 2R,\ \frac{c}{\sin {\angle C}}= 2Rsin‚àÝBb‚Äã=2R, sin‚àÝCc‚Äã=2R Hence: asin‚Å°‚àÝA=bsin‚Å°‚àÝB=csin‚Å°‚àÝC=2R‚ñÝ \frac{a}{\sin{\angle A}}=\frac{b}{\sin{\angle B}}=\frac{c}{\sin{\angle C}}= 2R \tag*{$\blacksquare$}sin‚àÝAa‚Äã=sin‚àÝBb‚Äã=sin‚àÝCc‚Äã=2R‚ñÝ

sin‚Å°(Œ±+Œ≤)=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤+cos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤\sin{\left(\alpha+\beta\right)}=\sin{\alpha}\cos{\beta}+\cos{\alpha}\sin{\beta}sin(Œ±+Œ≤)=sinŒ±cosŒ≤+cosŒ±sinŒ≤ Derivation sin‚Å°Œ≤=BCAB=BC1 ‚üπ BC=sin‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ≤=ACAB=AC1 ‚üπ AC=cos‚Å°Œ≤sin‚Å°Œ±=CDAC=CDcos‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ CD=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ±=CFBC=CFsin‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ CF=cos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{CD}{AC}=\frac{CD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies CD&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{CF}{BC}=\frac{CF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies CF&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinŒ≤=ABBC‚Äã=1BC‚Äã‚üπBCcosŒ≤=ABAC‚Äã=1AC‚Äã‚üπACsinŒ±=ACCD‚Äã=cosŒ≤CD‚Äã‚üπCDcosŒ±=BCCF‚Äã=sinŒ≤CF‚Äã‚üπCF‚Äã=sinŒ≤=cosŒ≤=sinŒ±cosŒ≤=cosŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äãsin‚Å°(Œ±+Œ≤)=BEAB=BE1=DF=CD+CF=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤+cos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \sin{(\green{\alpha}+\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{BE}{AB}=\frac{BE}{1}=DF\\ &=CD+CF\\ &=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}+\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}sin(Œ±+Œ≤)‚Äã=ABBE‚Äã=1BE‚Äã=DF=CD+CF=sinŒ±cosŒ≤+cosŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã

sin‚Å°(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤‚àícos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤\sin{\left(\alpha-\beta\right)}=\sin{\alpha}\cos{\beta}-\cos{\alpha}\sin{\beta}sin(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=sinŒ±cosŒ≤‚àícosŒ±sinŒ≤ Derivation sin‚Å°Œ≤=BCAB=BC1 ‚üπ BC=sin‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ≤=ACAB=AC1 ‚üπ AC=cos‚Å°Œ≤sin‚Å°Œ±=ADAC=ADcos‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ AD=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ±=BFBC=BFsin‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ BF=cos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{AD}{AC}=\frac{AD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies AD&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{BF}{BC}=\frac{BF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies BF&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinŒ≤=ABBC‚Äã=1BC‚Äã‚üπBCcosŒ≤=ABAC‚Äã=1AC‚Äã‚üπACsinŒ±=ACAD‚Äã=cosŒ≤AD‚Äã‚üπADcosŒ±=BCBF‚Äã=sinŒ≤BF‚Äã‚üπBF‚Äã=sinŒ≤=cosŒ≤=sinŒ±cosŒ≤=cosŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äãsin‚Å°(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=BGAB=BG1=AE=AD‚àíED=AD‚àíBF=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤‚àícos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \sin{(\green{\alpha}-\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{BG}{AB}=\frac{BG}{1}=AE\\ &=AD-ED\\ &=AD-BF\\ &=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}-\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}sin(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)‚Äã=ABBG‚Äã=1BG‚Äã=AE=AD‚àíED=AD‚àíBF=sinŒ±cosŒ≤‚àícosŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã

cos‚Å°(Œ±+Œ≤)=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤‚àísin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤\cos{\left(\alpha+\beta\right)}=\cos{\alpha}\cos{\beta}-\sin{\alpha}\sin{\beta}cos(Œ±+Œ≤)=cosŒ±cosŒ≤‚àísinŒ±sinŒ≤ Derivation sin‚Å°Œ≤=BCAB=BC1 ‚üπ BC=sin‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ≤=ACAB=AC1 ‚üπ AC=cos‚Å°Œ≤sin‚Å°Œ±=BFAC=BFsin‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ BF=sin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ±=ADBC=ADcos‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ AD=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{BF}{AC}=\frac{BF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies BF&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{AD}{BC}=\frac{AD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies AD&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinŒ≤=ABBC‚Äã=1BC‚Äã‚üπBCcosŒ≤=ABAC‚Äã=1AC‚Äã‚üπACsinŒ±=ACBF‚Äã=sinŒ≤BF‚Äã‚üπBFcosŒ±=BCAD‚Äã=cosŒ≤AD‚Äã‚üπAD‚Äã=sinŒ≤=cosŒ≤=sinŒ±sinŒ≤=cosŒ±cosŒ≤‚Äãcos‚Å°(Œ±+Œ≤)=AEAB=AE1=AD‚àíED=AD‚àíBF=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤‚àísin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \cos{(\green{\alpha}+\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{AE}{AB}=\frac{AE}{1}\\ &=AD-ED\\ &=AD-BF\\ &=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}-\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}cos(Œ±+Œ≤)‚Äã=ABAE‚Äã=1AE‚Äã=AD‚àíED=AD‚àíBF=cosŒ±cosŒ≤‚àísinŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã

cos‚Å°(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤+sin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤\cos{\left(\alpha-\beta\right)}=\cos{\alpha}\cos{\beta}+\sin{\alpha}\sin{\beta}cos(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=cosŒ±cosŒ≤+sinŒ±sinŒ≤ Derivation sin‚Å°Œ≤=BCAB=BC1 ‚üπ BC=sin‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ≤=ACAB=AC1 ‚üπ AC=cos‚Å°Œ≤sin‚Å°Œ±=CFBC=CFsin‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ CF=sin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤cos‚Å°Œ±=CDAC=CDcos‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ CD=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{CF}{BC}=\frac{CF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies CF&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{CD}{AC}=\frac{CD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies CD&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinŒ≤=ABBC‚Äã=1BC‚Äã‚üπBCcosŒ≤=ABAC‚Äã=1AC‚Äã‚üπACsinŒ±=BCCF‚Äã=sinŒ≤CF‚Äã‚üπCFcosŒ±=ACCD‚Äã=cosŒ≤CD‚Äã‚üπCD‚Äã=sinŒ≤=cosŒ≤=sinŒ±sinŒ≤=cosŒ±cosŒ≤‚Äãcos‚Å°(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=AGAB=AG1=DF=CD+CF=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ≤+sin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \cos{(\green{\alpha}-\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{AG}{AB}=\frac{AG}{1}=DF\\ &=CD+CF\\ &=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}+\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}cos(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)‚Äã=ABAG‚Äã=1AG‚Äã=DF=CD+CF=cosŒ±cosŒ≤+sinŒ±sinŒ≤‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã

tan‚Å°(Œ±+Œ≤)=tan‚Å°Œ±+tan‚Å°Œ≤1‚àítan‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤\tan{\left(\alpha+\beta\right)}=\frac{\tan{\alpha}+\tan{\beta}}{1-\tan{\alpha}\tan{\beta}}tan(Œ±+Œ≤)=1‚àítanŒ±tanŒ≤tanŒ±+tanŒ≤‚Äã Derivation tan‚Å°Œ±=CDAD=CD1 ‚üπ CD=tan‚Å°Œ±sec‚Å°Œ±=ACAD=AC1 ‚üπ AC=sec‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤=BCAC=BCsec‚Å°Œ± ‚üπ BC=sec‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤sec‚Å°Œ±=BCCF=sec‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤CF ‚üπ CF=tan‚Å°Œ≤tan‚Å°Œ±=BFCF=BFtan‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ BF=tan‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \green{\tan{\alpha}}=\frac{CD}{AD}=\frac{CD}{1}\implies CD&=\green{\tan{\alpha}}\\ \green{\sec{\alpha}}=\frac{AC}{AD}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\green{\sec{\alpha}}\\ \blue{\tan{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AC}=\frac{BC}{\green{\sec{\alpha}}}\implies BC&=\green{\sec{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}}\\ \green{\sec{\alpha}}=\frac{BC}{CF}=\frac{\green{\sec{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}}}{CF}\implies CF&=\blue{\tan{\beta}}\\ \green{\tan{\alpha}}=\frac{BF}{CF}=\frac{BF}{\blue{\tan{\beta}}}\implies BF&=\green{\tan{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}} \end{align*}tanŒ±=ADCD‚Äã=1CD‚Äã‚üπCDsecŒ±=ADAC‚Äã=1AC‚Äã‚üπACtanŒ≤=ACBC‚Äã=secŒ±BC‚Äã‚üπBCsecŒ±=CFBC‚Äã=CFsecŒ±tanŒ≤‚Äã‚üπCFtanŒ±=CFBF‚Äã=tanŒ≤BF‚Äã‚üπBF‚Äã=tanŒ±=secŒ±=secŒ±tanŒ≤=tanŒ≤=tanŒ±tanŒ≤‚Äãtan‚Å°(Œ±+Œ≤)=BEAE=DFAE=CD+CFAD‚àíBF=tan‚Å°Œ±+tan‚Å°Œ≤1‚àítan‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \tan{(\green{\alpha}+\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{BE}{AE}=\frac{DF}{AE}=\frac{CD+CF}{AD-BF}\\ &=\frac{\green{\tan{\alpha}}+\blue{\tan{\beta}}}{1-\green{\tan{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}tan(Œ±+Œ≤)‚Äã=AEBE‚Äã=AEDF‚Äã=AD‚àíBFCD+CF‚Äã=1‚àítanŒ±tanŒ≤tanŒ±+tanŒ≤‚Äã‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã

tan‚Å°(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=tan‚Å°Œ±‚àítan‚Å°Œ≤1+tan‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤\tan{\left(\alpha-\beta\right)}=\frac{\tan{\alpha}-\tan{\beta}}{1+\tan{\alpha}\tan{\beta}}tan(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=1+tanŒ±tanŒ≤tanŒ±‚àítanŒ≤‚Äã Derivation tan‚Å°Œ±=ADCD=AD1 ‚üπ AD=tan‚Å°Œ±sec‚Å°Œ±=ACCD=AC1 ‚üπ AC=sec‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤=BCAC=BCsec‚Å°Œ± ‚üπ BC=sec‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤sec‚Å°Œ±=BCBF=sec‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤BF ‚üπ BF=tan‚Å°Œ≤tan‚Å°Œ±=CFBF=CFtan‚Å°Œ≤ ‚üπ CF=tan‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \green{\tan{\alpha}}=\frac{AD}{CD}=\frac{AD}{1}\implies AD&=\green{\tan{\alpha}}\\ \green{\sec{\alpha}}=\frac{AC}{CD}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\green{\sec{\alpha}}\\ \blue{\tan{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AC}=\frac{BC}{\green{\sec{\alpha}}}\implies BC&=\green{\sec{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}}\\ \green{\sec{\alpha}}=\frac{BC}{BF}=\frac{\green{\sec{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}}}{BF}\implies BF&=\blue{\tan{\beta}}\\ \green{\tan{\alpha}}=\frac{CF}{BF}=\frac{CF}{\blue{\tan{\beta}}}\implies CF&=\green{\tan{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}} \end{align*}tanŒ±=CDAD‚Äã=1AD‚Äã‚üπADsecŒ±=CDAC‚Äã=1AC‚Äã‚üπACtanŒ≤=ACBC‚Äã=secŒ±BC‚Äã‚üπBCsecŒ±=BFBC‚Äã=BFsecŒ±tanŒ≤‚Äã‚üπBFtanŒ±=BFCF‚Äã=tanŒ≤CF‚Äã‚üπCF‚Äã=tanŒ±=secŒ±=secŒ±tanŒ≤=tanŒ≤=tanŒ±tanŒ≤‚Äãtan‚Å°(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)=BGAG=AEDF=AD‚àíBFCD+CF=tan‚Å°Œ±‚àítan‚Å°Œ≤1+tan‚Å°Œ±tan‚Å°Œ≤ \begin{align*} \tan{(\green{\alpha}-\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{BG}{AG}=\frac{AE}{DF}=\frac{AD-BF}{CD+CF}\\ &=\frac{\green{\tan{\alpha}}-\blue{\tan{\beta}}}{1+\green{\tan{\alpha}}\blue{\tan{\beta}}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}tan(Œ±‚àíŒ≤)‚Äã=AGBG‚Äã=DFAE‚Äã=CD+CFAD‚àíBF‚Äã=1+tanŒ±tanŒ≤tanŒ±‚àítanŒ≤‚Äã‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã

sin‚Å°2Œ±=2sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±\sin{2\alpha}=2\sin{\alpha}\cos{\alpha}sin2Œ±=2sinŒ±cosŒ± Derivation sin‚Å°2Œ±=sin‚Å°(Œ±+Œ±)=sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±+cos‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ±(Trig Identity #4)=2sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±\begin{align*} \sin{2\alpha}&=\sin{({\alpha}+{\alpha})}\\ &=\sin{{\alpha}}\cos{{\alpha}}+\cos{{\alpha}}\sin{{\alpha}} &&({\text{Trig Identity \#4}})\\ &=2\sin{\alpha}\cos{\alpha} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}sin2Œ±‚Äã=sin(Œ±+Œ±)=sinŒ±cosŒ±+cosŒ±sinŒ±=2sinŒ±cosŒ±‚Äã‚Äã(Trig Identity #4)‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã Trig Identity #4

cos‚Å°2Œ±=cos‚Å°2Œ±‚àísin‚Å°2Œ±=2cos‚Å°2Œ±‚àí1=1‚àí2sin‚Å°2Œ±\cos{2\alpha}=\cos^2{\alpha}-\sin^2{\alpha}=2\cos^2{\alpha}-1=1-2\sin^2{\alpha}cos2Œ±=cos2Œ±‚àísin2Œ±=2cos2Œ±‚àí1=1‚àí2sin2Œ± Derivation cos‚Å°2Œ±=cos‚Å°(Œ±+Œ±)=cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±‚àísin‚Å°Œ±sin‚Å°Œ±(Trig Identity #6)=cos‚Å°2Œ±‚àísin‚Å°2Œ±=cos‚Å°2Œ±‚àí(1‚àícos‚Å°2Œ±)=2cos‚Å°2Œ±‚àí1(Trig Identity #1)=(1‚àísin‚Å°2Œ±)‚àísin‚Å°2Œ±=1‚àí2sin‚Å°2Œ±(Trig Identity #1)\begin{align*} \cos{2\alpha}&=\cos{({\alpha}+{\alpha})}\\ &=\cos{{\alpha}}\cos{{\alpha}}-\sin{{\alpha}}\sin{{\alpha}} &&({\text{Trig Identity \#6}})\\ &=\cos^2{\alpha}-\sin^2{\alpha}\\ &=\cos^2{\alpha}-(1-\cos^2{\alpha})=2\cos^2{\alpha}-1 &&({\text{Trig Identity \#1}})\\ &=(1-\sin^2{\alpha})-\sin^2{\alpha}=1-2\sin^2{\alpha}&&({\text{Trig Identity \#1}}) \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}cos2Œ±‚Äã=cos(Œ±+Œ±)=cosŒ±cosŒ±‚àísinŒ±sinŒ±=cos2Œ±‚àísin2Œ±=cos2Œ±‚àí(1‚àícos2Œ±)=2cos2Œ±‚àí1=(1‚àísin2Œ±)‚àísin2Œ±=1‚àí2sin2Œ±‚Äã‚Äã(Trig Identity #6)(Trig Identity #1)(Trig Identity #1)‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã Trig Identity #6 Trig Identity #1

sin‚Å°3Œ±=‚àí4sin‚Å°3Œ±+3sin‚Å°Œ±\sin{3\alpha}=-4\sin^3{\alpha}+3\sin{\alpha}sin3Œ±=‚àí4sin3Œ±+3sinŒ± Derivation sin‚Å°3Œ±=sin‚Å°(2Œ±+Œ±)=sin‚Å°2Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±+cos‚Å°2Œ±sin‚Å°Œ±(Trig Identity #4)=2sin‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±cos‚Å°Œ±+cos‚Å°2Œ±sin‚Å°Œ±(Trig Identity #10)=2sin‚Å°Œ±(cos‚Å°2Œ±)+(1‚àí2sin‚Å°2Œ±)sin‚Å°Œ±(Trig Identity #11)=2sin‚Å°Œ±(1‚àísin‚Å°2Œ±)+(1‚àí2sin‚Å°2Œ±)sin‚Å°Œ±(Trig Identity #1)=2sin‚Å°Œ±‚àí2sin‚Å°3Œ±+sin‚Å°Œ±‚àí2sin‚Å°3Œ±=‚àí4sin‚Å°3Œ±+3sin‚Å°Œ±\begin{align*} \sin{3\alpha}&=\sin{({2\alpha}+{\alpha})}\\ &=\sin{{2\alpha}}\cos{{\alpha}}+\cos{{2\alpha}}\sin{{\alpha}} &&({\text{Trig Identity \#4}})\\ &=2\sin{\alpha}\cos{\alpha}\cos{\alpha}+\cos{{2\alpha}}\sin{{\alpha}} &&({\text{Trig Identity \#10}})\\ &=2\sin{\alpha}(\cos^2{\alpha})+(1-2\sin^2{\alpha})\sin{{\alpha}} &&({\text{Trig Identity \#11}})\\ &=2\sin{\alpha}(1-\sin^2{\alpha})+(1-2\sin^2{\alpha})\sin{{\alpha}} &&({\text{Trig Identity \#1}})\\ &=2\sin{\alpha}-2\sin^3{\alpha}+\sin{\alpha}-2\sin^3{\alpha}\\ &=-4\sin^3{\alpha}+3\sin{\alpha} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}sin3Œ±‚Äã=sin(2Œ±+Œ±)=sin2Œ±cosŒ±+cos2Œ±sinŒ±=2sinŒ±cosŒ±cosŒ±+cos2Œ±sinŒ±=2sinŒ±(cos2Œ±)+(1‚àí2sin2Œ±)sinŒ±=2sinŒ±(1‚àísin2Œ±)+(1‚àí2sin2Œ±)sinŒ±=2sinŒ±‚àí2sin3Œ±+sinŒ±‚àí2sin3Œ±=‚àí4sin3Œ±+3sinŒ±‚Äã‚Äã(Trig Identity #4)(Trig Identity #10)(Trig Identity #11)(Trig Identity #1)‚Äã‚ñÝ‚Äã Trig Identity #4 \quad Trig Identity #10 Trig Identity #11 \quad Trig Identity #1

This section draws largely from the author's summer 2025 experience at Stanford University in the course STATS 117, alongside the open-source text"book" The Art of Chance. Rather than building Probability from pure theory, this section focuses on how it's actually applied.

Fundamental Principle of Counting Suppose nnn decisions are made in sequence, where at the iiith step there are kik_iki‚Äã available options. If each decision is made independently (meaning the outcome of one will not influence or be influenced by any other), then the total number of unique ways to perform all nnn decisions is ‚àèi=1nki=k1√ók2√ó‚ãØ√ókn\prod_{i=1}^{n}k_{i}=k_1\times k_2\times \cdots \times k_ni=1‚àèn‚Äãki‚Äã=k1‚Äã√ók2‚Äã√ó‚ãØ√ókn‚Äã

There are four counting methods, depending on two questions: does order matter? And can choices repeat? When order matters, we call it Permutations; on the contrary, when order does not matters, we call it Combinations. When choice repeat, we call it with Replacement; on the contrary, when choice does not repeat, we call it without Replacement.

Permutations with replacement If there are nnn unique items, then the number of Permutations to select kkk items with replacement is: nkn^k nk Derivation By the Fundamental Principle of Counting, this is just kkk independent steps, where at each step there are nnn choices avaliable (since replacement means there is nothing moved from the pool). Therefore, the number of Permutations is : n√ón√ó‚ãØ√ón‚èüktimes=nk‚ñÝ\underbrace{n\times n\times \cdots \times n}_{k\text{times}}=n^k \tag*{$\blacksquare$}ktimesn√ón√ó‚ãØ√ón‚Äã‚Äã=nk‚ñÝ

Permutations without Replacement If there are nnn unique items, then the number of Permutations to select kkk items without replacement is: n!(n‚àík)!\frac{n!}{(n-k)!}(n‚àík)!n!‚Äã Derivation By the Fundamental Principle of Counting, this is just kkk independent steps, but since items are not replaced, the pool shrinks by one at each step: nnn choice in step 1, n‚àí1n-1n‚àí1 choice in step two, and so on down to n‚àík+1n-k+1n‚àík+1 in step kkk. Therefore, the number of Permutations is : n√ó(n‚àí1)√ó(n‚àí2)√ó‚ãØ√ó(n‚àík+1)=n!(n‚àík)!‚ñÝn\times (n-1)\times (n-2)\times \cdots\times (n-k+1) =\frac{n!}{(n-k)!} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}n√ó(n‚àí1)√ó(n‚àí2)√ó‚ãØ√ó(n‚àík+1)=(n‚àík)!n!‚Äã‚ñÝ

Combinations without Replacement If there are nnn unique items, then the number of Combinations to select kkk items without replacement is denoted (nk)\binom{n}{k}(kn‚Äã), read as "n choose k", and defined as: (nk)=defn!k!(n‚àík)!\binom{n}{k}\overset{\text{def}}{=}\frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}(kn‚Äã)=defk!(n‚àík)!n!‚Äã This could also be found in [Binomial Theorem] Derivation Start with permutation without Replacement, and the number of Permutations to select kkk items from nnn without replacement is: n!(n‚àík)!\frac{n!}{(n-k)!}(n‚àík)!n!‚Äã But since order doesn't matter, every group of kkk items gets counted k!k!k! times, so we divide out the overcounting and get: n!(n‚àík)!√∑k!=n!k!(n‚àík)!=(nk)‚ñÝ\frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\div {k!}=\frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}=\binom{n}{k} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}(n‚àík)!n!‚Äã√∑k!=k!(n‚àík)!n!‚Äã=(kn‚Äã)‚ñÝ

Combinations with Replacement If there are nnn unique items, then the number of Combinations to select kkk items with replacement is: (n+k‚àí1n‚àí1)=(n+k‚àí1k)\binom{n+k-1}{n-1}=\binom{n+k-1}{k}(n‚àí1n+k‚àí1‚Äã)=(kn+k‚àí1‚Äã) Derivation This scenario is a little tricky, so we will follow an example: Pirates split coins 3 pirates are picking 10 identical coins, where each pirate can receive zero or more coins. How many ways can this be done? Solution This is a Combinations with Replacement problem because the coins are identical so order doesn't matter (Combination), and each pirate can receive more than one coin (with Replacement). Instead of directly counting distributions among pirates, we can transform 3 pirates into 2 partitions that split the coins, shown as: ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚à£‚èüpartition‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚à£‚èüpartition‚ãÜ‚ãÜ\star\star\star \underbrace{|}_{\text{partition}} \star\star\star\star \star \underbrace{|}_{\text{partition}} \star\star‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜpartition‚à£‚Äã‚Äã‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜ‚ãÜpartition‚à£‚Äã‚Äã‚ãÜ‚ãÜ Then, the problem just became Combinations without Replacement that choose 2 positions for partitions among all 12 positions (or choose 10 positions for coins among all 12 positions), which is: (122)=(1210)=66\binom{12}{2}=\binom{12}{10}=66(212‚Äã)=(1012‚Äã)=66 Correspondingly, when nnn pirates pick kkk identical coins, the number of Combinations with replacement is: (n+k‚àí1n‚àí1)=(n+k‚àí1k)‚ñÝ\binom{n+k-1}{n-1}=\binom{n+k-1}{k} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}(n‚àí1n+k‚àí1‚Äã)=(kn+k‚àí1‚Äã)‚ñÝ

Let's discover Docusaurus in less than 5 minutes.
Get started by creating a new site. Or try Docusaurus immediately with docusaurus.new.
Node.js version 20.0 or above: When installing Node.js, you are recommended to check all checkboxes related to dependencies.

Generate a new Docusaurus site using the classic template. The classic template will automatically be added to your project after you run the command: npm init docusaurus@latest my-website classic You can type this command into Command Prompt, Powershell, Terminal, or any other integrated terminal of your code editor. The command also installs all necessary dependencies you need to run Docusaurus.

Run the development server: cd my-website npm run start The cd command changes the directory you're working with. In order to work with your newly created Docusaurus site, you'll need to navigate the terminal there. The npm run start command builds your website locally and serves it through a development server, ready for you to view at http://localhost:3000/. Open docs/intro.md (this page) and edit some lines: the site reloads automatically and displays your changes.
